title: Today's Tech Brief: The skeptic’s guide to humanoid robots going viral on the Internet
summary: A look at humanoid robots going viral and California's exemption for Linux on age checks
imageAlt: Image from source article for The skeptic’s guide to humanoid robots going viral on the Internet
imageCaption: The skeptic’s guide to humanoid robots going viral on the Internet
imageSourceTitle: The skeptic’s guide to humanoid robots going viral on the Internet

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to today's tech brief. We're diving into the world of humanoid robots and a curious update from California on Linux. Let's get started. I've been following these developments, and I have to say, they're both fascinating and a bit surprising.

First up, humanoid robots are going viral on the internet. It seems like these robots capable of handling almost any task have taken the web by storm. But, as Ars Technica points out, there are reasons to be skeptical about their capabilities and the tasks they can truly handle. I've been looking at images of these robots doing tasks that seem almost human-like, but the question remains, are they really as capable as they seem? It's like they're trying to fool us into thinking they're more advanced than they actually are.

Next, California may let Linux bypass age checks. According to The Register, there's an exemption in an amendment that offers relief to open-source software makers. This could be a big win for Linux and other open-source software, giving them more flexibility in the state. I think this is a great example of how regulatory shifts can have a significant impact on the tech landscape.

And, there's another story worth mentioning - the intersection of tech and media. It seems like the lines between technology and entertainment are blurring, with AI-generated content becoming more prevalent. It's an interesting time to be following the tech industry, that's for sure.

My quick read: today's tech stories are less about one giant thunderclap and more about a lively weather pattern. A little platform drama here, a little artificial intelligence irony there, and suddenly the industry has enough material for a very caffeinated morning show.
